result.book_thesis: The quality of written prose is determined by objective linguistic and psychological principles—specifically the principle of 'relative readability'—rather than ideology or personal taste, as the functional requirements of 'context-free' written speech drive a universal historical tendency toward communicative efficiency.

core_frameworks.name: Linguistics of Literacy
core_frameworks.description: A study of written speech as a distinct functional system rather than a mere secondary representation of oral language.

components.name: Functional Distinction
components.description: The requirement for written speech to be 'context-free' or self-contextualizing due to the absence of immediate situational clues.

components.name: Grapholect Stability
components.description: The concept that standard written languages (grapholects) are stable, normative systems distinct from evolving oral dialects.

core_frameworks.name: Relative Readability
core_frameworks.description: A stylistic principle defining prose excellence as the communication of intended meaning with the least possible reader effort.

components.name: Semantic Intention
components.description: The realization that 'easiness' must be measured relative to the specific meaning an author intends to convey.

components.name: Communicative Efficiency
components.description: The historical and psychological tendency of language to evolve toward reduced cognitive processing costs.

core_frameworks.name: Bloomfieldian Phoneticism
core_frameworks.description: The doctrine that writing is merely a secondary way of recording 'real' (oral) language.

components.name: Phonetic Primacy
components.description: The assumption that sound is the primary medium of language and writing has no independent authority.

chapter_previews.chapter: Chapter 1: The Normative Character of Written Speech
chapter_previews.predicted_main_argument: Written speech is a separate, potentially independent symbolic system that must supply its own context to be communicative.

chapter_previews.chapter: Chapter 2: Progressive Tendencies in the History of Language and of Prose
chapter_previews.predicted_main_argument: Standard written languages (grapholects) are inherently conservative and normative, moving toward universal stability rather than dialectal change.

chapter_previews.chapter: Chapter 3: Refining the Concept of Readability
chapter_previews.predicted_main_argument: The history of prose demonstrates a universal progressive tendency toward increased communicative efficiency across all genres.

chapter_previews.chapter: Chapter 4: The Psychological Bases of Readability
chapter_previews.predicted_main_argument: Readability is grounded in psychological universals, particularly the constraints of short-term and long-term memory.
Current knowledge of psycholinguistic principles allows for specific, non-speculative improvements in composition pedagogy.

chapter_previews.chapter: Chapter 6: The Valid Assessment of Writing Ability
chapter_previews.predicted_main_argument: A valid and reliable system for rating prose quality must be based on the objective criterion of relative readability.

key_terms.definition: The ability to write extended, communicative discourse, going beyond mere reading or letter formation.
key_terms.source_passage: In this book, I use the word to embrace also the ability to write extended, communicative discourse.

key_terms.definition: A standard written language that has become different in kind from oral dialects due to its stability and lack of grammatical change.
key_terms.source_passage: Some sociolinguists have therefore coined the term grapholect to indicate that standard written languages are different in kind from oral dialects.

key_terms.definition: The principle that one prose style is superior to another if it conveys the same meaning with less reader effort, varying according to the semantic intention.
key_terms.source_passage: One prose style is better than another when it communicates the same meanings as the other does but requires less effort from the reader.

key_terms.definition: The requirement for written speech to provide its own internal context because it lacks the external, extra-verbal clues of oral speech.
key_terms.source_passage: The functional peculiarity of writing is its need to furnish its own context.

key_terms.definition: A form of speech that is not context-tied, where the burden of meaning inheres predominantly in the verbal channel.
key_terms.source_passage: The speech shows careful editing at both the grammatical and lexical levels; it is no longer context-tied.

intellectual_genealogy.name: Henry Bradley
intellectual_genealogy.contribution: Established the primacy of writing as an independent symbolic system in literate societies.

intellectual_genealogy.name: Leonard Bloomfield
intellectual_genealogy.contribution: Argued that writing is merely a secondary representation of oral speech, a view the author seeks to correct.

intellectual_genealogy.name: Herbert Spencer
intellectual_genealogy.contribution: Authored the first extensive discussion of the principle of economy of reader attention/readability.

intellectual_genealogy.name: Ferdinand de Saussure
intellectual_genealogy.contribution: The author utilizes Saussure’s distinction between 'parole' (speech) and 'langue' (language system) to categorize written speech.

intellectual_genealogy.name: Basil Bernstein
intellectual_genealogy.contribution: Provided the distinction between 'restricted' and 'elaborated' codes, which the author maps onto oral vs. written functions.

intellectual_genealogy.name: Karl Marx
intellectual_genealogy.contribution: The author adopts the logical structure of the Marxist historical argument regarding inevitable/desirable historical tendencies.
